inputs.label: Base Metal Thickness
inputs.businessContext: Thickness of the base metal to be welded. Per AWS D1.1 and ISO 15609-1, preheat temperature depends on thickness.

inputs.label: Carbon Equivalent (CE)
inputs.businessContext: Carbon equivalent value calculated from material composition. Based on IIW formula: CE = C + Mn/6 + (Cr+Mo+V)/5 + (Ni+Cu)/15. Higher CE requires higher preheat.

inputs.label: Hydrogen Level
inputs.businessContext: Hydrogen content in weld metal per ISO 3690. Low: <5 ml/100g, Medium: 5-10 ml/100g, High: >10 ml/100g. Affects preheat to avoid hydrogen cracking.

inputs.label: Heat Input
inputs.businessContext: Heat input during welding. Calculated as (Voltage * Current * 60) / Travel Speed. Lower heat input may require higher preheat.

inputs.label: Restraint Degree
inputs.businessContext: Degree of joint restraint. High restraint increases risk of cracking, requiring higher preheat. Per AWS D1.1.

inputs.label: Ambient Temperature
inputs.businessContext: Temperature of the surrounding environment. Lower ambient temperature may require higher preheat.

rules: baseMetalThickness must be between 1 and 200 mm
heatInput must be between 0.5 and 5.0 kJ/mm

thresholds.preheatTemperature: If preheatTemperature > 250, warning: 'High preheat temperature may cause metallurgical issues.'
thresholds.carbonEquivalent: If carbonEquivalent > 0.45, warning: 'High carbon equivalent increases cracking risk.'

hiddenLossDrivers: carbonEquivalent > 0.45 ? 'High carbon equivalent increases cracking risk' : ''
preheatTemperature > 250 ? 'High preheat temperature may cause metallurgical issues' : ''
suggestedActions: If preheat temperature is too high, consider using low hydrogen welding consumables.
If carbon equivalent is high, consider preheating to at least 150°C.
Ensure ambient temperature is above 10°C to reduce preheat requirements.
